title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: LCFS Sees Price Resilience Following Key Amendments to the Program
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(11th November â€“ 17th November)
  • A total of 227,132 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 29.80% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades recorded a fall of 10.90% WoW as 425,704 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price increased this week to $70.24/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 6.30%Â rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 5.63% in price reaching $69.19/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 7.99% WoW; on 19th Nov, it was $77.38/MT. The benchmark price stood at $78.00/MT on 19th
  • D3 RIN prices fell by 6.19% WoW, while D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices each increased by 1.37% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Prices in the LCFS market have experienced a significant rise, reflecting the broader impact of recent regulatory developments. Following CARB's approval of extensive amendments to the Low Carbon Fuel Standard on 8th November, spot prices surged to $76.75 by 11th Novemberâ€”the highest level since 1st September 2023. This demonstrates the marketâ€™s positive response to the anticipated long-term effects of these updates, which are set to take effect in Q1 2025. Key changes include raising the carbon intensity reduction target to 30% by 2030 and 90% by 2045, phasing out credits for biomethane used in combustion engines to prioritize renewable hydrogen, and implementing stricter safeguards against deforestation. These measures aim to align with California's carbon neutrality goals while catalyzing investments in zero-emission infrastructure. The amendments also address equity concerns by increasing incentives for underserved communities and ensuring sustainable biofuel sourcing. This regulatory clarity has boosted confidence in the program, driving higher credit prices as market participants adjust to the updated framework. For more details on the LCFS amendments, read here.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_589719" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 6.30% WoW rise from $66.08 to $70.24 this week. Additionally, all non-zero credit prices rose by 5.63% compared to the previous week. Type 1 trades saw 227,132 credits exchanged, recording a rise of 29.80% WoW this week, but the total transaction volume for All Non-Zero Trades had decreased by 10.90% WoW this week, to reach 425,704 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_589720" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 7.99% WoW, reaching $76.65/MT. On 19th Nov, the average spot price was $77.38/MT, which rose from $60.67/MT, the lowest point in October. The benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $78.00/MT as of 19th Nov, up from $61.38/MT as of 23rdÂ Oct.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_589721" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 47 trades were reported in the market, with three high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $77.00 for three transactions of Type 1 at a volume of 2,500, 10,000 and 20,000 credits, and the lowest transfer by price last week was at $46.50 per credit of Type 1, reported at a volume of 5,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 30,000 credits in Type 2 were undertaken for price of $70.00, and the smallest volume transfers of 1,472 credits, a Type 2 category, were undertaken for price of $63.50.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_589722" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] October saw strong momentum with volumes reaching 6.40 MMT, a remarkable 66.23% increase year-on-year from 3.85 MMT in October 2023. This represents the highest monthly transaction volume in 2024, outperforming all earlier months. Meanwhile, the average Type 1 price climbed to $68.43 as of 15th November, with volumes totalling 0.92 MMT by mid-November, reflecting sustained market resilience. For comparison, prices in October 2023 stood at approximately $70.12.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_589723" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 11th Â Â Nov â€“ 17th Nov, total VPT recorded a 7.83% rise (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices fell by 6.19% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 1.37% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • CARB Updates the Low Carbon Fuel Standard to Increase Access to Cleaner Fuels and Zero-Emission Transportation Options. The California Air Resources Board approved updates to the Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) that channel global, national and local private sector investment towards increasing cleaner fuel and transportation options for consumers, accelerating the deployment of zero-emission infrastructure, and keeping the state on track to meet legislatively mandated air quality and climate targets.
  • RFA Welcomes Legislation Extending Second-Generation Biofuels Tax Credit. The Renewable Fuels Association thanked a bipartisan duo of lawmakers who introduced legislation to extend the Second Generation Biofuel Producer Tax Credit for an additional year. 2022â€™s Inflation Reduction Act extended this credit until the end of 2024, and this new bill would extend it through 2025, allowing time for the federal government to complete its work on another key credit to promote lower-carbon fuels, RFA noted. The House bill was introduced by Rep. Mariannette Miller-Meeks (R-IA) and Sharice Davids (D-KS).
  • Montana Renewables to Add 150 MMgy of SAF Capacity by 2026. Calumet Inc. reported third-quarter results and shared updates on the MaxSAF initiative by Montana Renewables, supported by a conditional loan guarantee from the U.S. Department of Energy of up to $1.44 billion. This funding will aid in expanding Montana Renewablesâ€™ biorefinery in Great Falls, Montana, aiming to make it one of the worldâ€™s largest producers of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), with a projected capacity of 300 million gallons per year (MMgy) for SAF and 330 MMgy for combined SAF and renewable diesel.
  • Neste and Air Canada Sign Agreement for the Supply of 60,000 tons of Neste MY Sustainable Aviation Fuel â€“ the First Time Nesteâ€™s SAF is Supplied to Canada. Neste and Air Canada have signed an agreement for the supply of 60,000 metric tons (77.6 million liters) of neat Neste MY Sustainable Aviation Fuelâ„¢. Neste will deliver the sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) blended with conventional jet fuel to the Vancouver marine terminal starting in December 2024, with further shipments throughout 2025. The Vancouver marine terminal has a direct pipeline connection to the fuel facilities at Vancouver International Airport enabling the use of the blended SAF at the airport.
  • U.S. Department of Energy Releases New Report on Pathways to Commercial Liftoff for Sustainable Aviation Fuel. The U.S. Department of Energy's new "Pathways to Commercial Liftoff" report highlights the potential for sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) to decarbonize aviation, with over three billion gallons of annual SAF production capacity expected by 2030. Meeting this goal requires scaling current SAF technologies, reducing high costs through federal and state incentives, and ensuring long-term offtake agreements. The report emphasizes international policy alignment on carbon accounting and standards. DOEâ€™s recent $1.44 billion and $1.46 billion loan commitments to SAF. facilities in Montana and South Dakota aim to accelerate SAF production, advancing the U.S. as a leader in clean aviation fuel.
  • Proposed Rule by EPA on RFS Program Partial Waiver of 2024 Cellulosic Biofuel Volume Requirement and Extension of 2024 Compliance Deadline Under Review in OBM. On 12th November, the U.S. EPA submitted a proposed rule on Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) Program: Partial Waiver of 2024 Cellulosic Biofuel Volume Requirement and Extension of 2024 Compliance Deadline to the White House Office of Budget and Management. This follows a petition filed by the AFPM to the EPA earlier in the month.
